speaker: ALEX
text: This is Git Going with GitHub, episode 38: Resources and Links. I am Alex. By the end of this episode, Resources and Links should feel less like a wall of GitHub words and more like a set of moves you can trust.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I am here for the practical questions: what should I listen for, what can go wrong, and what is the next calm move?

speaker: ALEX
text: Today we are working on this: Tools, references, communities, and continued learning paths. I want the learner to leave with a mental map, not just a remembered path through buttons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal is understanding first, then action, then confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. We are building a rhythm: orient yourself, take one intentional action, then verify what changed before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does someone need before they touch the keyboard?

speaker: ALEX
text: Everything You Need - Before, During, and After the Workshop: Every link, tool, guide, and community resource from the two-day workshop in one place. The next useful detail is concrete: Bookmark this page in your fork so it travels with you.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next layer is this. Learning Cards: Navigating This Resource Guide has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here are the anchors worth keeping. This appendix is organized as 16 numbered sections with heading levels -- press H or 2 to jump between major sections in browse mode. Most sections contain tables of links -- navigate tables with T to jump to the next table, then use arrow keys to read rows and columns. Each resource link opens in the same tab by default -- use Ctrl+Enter (Windows) to open in a new tab and keep this reference page available. Tables in this appendix have a Resource/URL/Notes column structure -- widen your browser or zoom out slightly if columns overlap at high magnification. Link text is descriptive (resource names, not raw URLs) making it easier to scan the page visually. Bookmark this page in your browser for quick access -- it is the single-page reference for everything from the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 1. The Central Project - Accessibility Agents: the project you forked, contributed to, and carry home.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Anchor this part on Your Personal Fork. After the workshop, your fork lives at.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Quick access from VS Code has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is clone your fork: git clone https://github.com/[your-username]/accessibility-agents.git. Step two is open in VS Code: cd accessibility-agents && code. Step three is open Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Alt+I, or run Chat: Open Chat from the Command Palette if your keymap differs. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: Personalizing Your Fork has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, copy preferences.example.md to preferences.md in.github/agents/. Then, add your GitHub username, your most-used repositories, and your preferred output format. After that, commit the file - now the agents know who you are and what you work on. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 2. GitHub Accessibility Guides is still useful because official guides from the GitHub Accessibility team. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: These were the primary research sources for this workshop's documentation.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put 3. GitHub Skills Learning Modules into plain language. GitHub Skills is GitHub's free, self-paced interactive learning platform. The useful version is: Every course runs entirely inside GitHub - no external site, no separate login, no video to watch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Unlike a conventional course where you watch videos or read slides, GitHub Skills teaches through doing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Copy the course: Select "Start course" → "Use this template" → "Create a new repository." This copies the course scaffold to your own account; Mona activates: A GitHub Actions workflow automatically runs - within 20 seconds, Mona opens your first lesson as an Issue in your new repository; Read and act: The issue contains step-by-step instructions. You do the task (commit a file, open a PR, resolve a conflict) in the same repository; and Mona validates: Another GitHub Actions workflow detects what you did, checks if it's correct, and either advances you to the next step or gives you feedback to try again. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is repeat until done: All feedback arrives as issue comments and new issues. The course is complete when Mona closes the final issue with a success message. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because since everything happens in GitHub, the accessibility skills from this workshop apply directly. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should they picture the shape of the workshop?

speaker: ALEX
text: After This Workshop - Your Learning Path: GitHub Skills courses are available 24/7 and are completely free. The next useful detail is concrete: Recommended order after this workshop.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Learning Cards: Screen Reader Downloads and Setup has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part that makes the next action easier. NVDA (free, Windows): download from nvaccess.org -- after installing, toggle browse/focus mode with NVDA+Space and open the elements list with NVDA+F7. JAWS (Windows): download a trial from freedomscientific.com -- Virtual PC Cursor toggle is Insert+Z, elements list is Insert+F3. VoiceOver (macOS/iOS): built in, no download needed -- start with Cmd+F5 on Mac, or Settings then Accessibility then VoiceOver on iOS. NVDA and JAWS both support speech and braille output simultaneously if you use a refreshable braille display. VoiceOver on macOS integrates with Zoom (screen magnifier) -- enable both in System Settings then Accessibility for combined magnification and speech. Narrator on Windows is built in and requires no download -- launch with Win+Ctrl+Enter for a quick, lightweight screen reader experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There are a lot of tools in play. How do we keep that from feeling like a contest?

speaker: ALEX
text: Key commands (quick reference) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the pieces that turn the idea into a usable move. Toggle browse/focus mode: NVDA+Space. Elements list: NVDA+F7. Next heading: H Next link: K Next button: B Next form field: F.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the moment where the page starts to make sense. Key commands (quick reference) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Listen for the small confirmations in this list. Virtual PC Cursor on/off: Insert+Z. Elements list: Insert+F3. Next heading: H Next link: Tab or U Next button: B.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What stays the same when the tool changes?

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful version is not abstract; it sounds like this. Start/stop VoiceOver: Cmd+F5. VO modifier: Caps Lock or Ctrl+Option. Rotor: VO+U. Next heading: VO+Cmd+H.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Install the GitHub PR extension quickly has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, open VS Code Extensions (Ctrl+Shift+X). Then, search: GitHub Pull Requests. After that, install: publisher is "GitHub". Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 7. GitHub Agentic Workflows is still useful because GitHub Agentic Workflows are in technical preview as of February 2026. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Access, feedback channels, and setup information.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How Agentic Workflows connect to what you learned. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put How Agentic Workflows connect to what you learned into plain language. The only difference is where they run and how sophisticated their executor is. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Standard GitHub Actions (YAML workflows - triggers, jobs, steps); Accessibility Agents agents (.agent.md files - plain English instructions, Copilot Chat executor); and GitHub Agentic Workflows (.md files in.github/workflows/ - plain English instructions, cloud-based coding agent executor). The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The core idea: Write the intent of a feature before anyone builds it. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: The specification is a living document - AI uses it to plan tasks, contributors use it to stay aligned, the community uses it to evaluate whether the outcome matched the intention.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because works with GitHub Copilot, Claude Code, and Gemini CLI.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. /specify - open a new specification session. /plan - convert a spec into a development plan. /tasks - break the plan into trackable tasks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should they understand before typing anything?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub CLI (gh) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Install; winget install GitHub.cli Windows; brew install gh macOS; Most useful commands for contributors; gh issue list List issues in current repo; gh issue view 42 Read issue 42; gh pr list List open PRs; gh pr view 14 Read PR 14; gh pr create Create a PR. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you keep commands from becoming magic words?

speaker: ALEX
text: Copilot in the CLI (gh copilot) has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Install the extension; gh extension install github/gh-copilot; Ask Copilot to explain a command; gh copilot explain "git rebase -i HEAD 3"; Ask Copilot to suggest a command; gh copilot suggest "undo my last commit but keep the changes staged". Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 10. GitHub Mobile Apps: GitHub's official mobile apps bring the full GitHub experience to your phone or tablet. That matters in practice: Perfect for reviewing PRs, triaging issues, and staying connected when away from your computer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. What You Can Do in GitHub Mobile has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Browse repositories - navigate code, read files, view commits. Manage issues - create, edit, comment, close, and label issues. Review pull requests - read diffs, leave comments, request changes, approve, merge. Manage notifications - triage your inbox on the go. Interact with GitHub Copilot - chat with Copilot Chat on mobile (as of Feb 2026). View GitHub Actions - monitor workflows and check build status.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: pro tip: Enable push notifications for mentions and reviews so you can respond quickly when your input is needed.

speaker: ALEX
text: These are the details that keep the idea from floating away. Native screen reader gestures (VoiceOver on iOS, TalkBack on Android). Dynamic text sizing. Dark mode / high contrast. Keyboard navigation (when using external keyboard with tablet).

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat 11. GitHub Best Practices and Power Features as decoration. Essential tips and lesser-known features that make you a more effective contributor. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Saved Replies. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Saved Replies is still useful because saved replies let you create reusable text templates for common responses.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Thanking first-time contributors. Requesting more information with a friendly tone. Explaining common setup issues. Closing duplicate issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to set up. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to set up has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Go to github.com/settings/replies; Click "Add a saved reply"; Give it a short label (e.g., "welcome-first-time"); and Write your template text (can include Markdown). If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to use. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to use has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. When writing any comment, press Ctrl+. (period) to open the saved replies menu. Select your saved reply. Edit as needed before posting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Put that beside the next piece. This part earns its place because pin important issues to the top of your repository's Issues tab. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. FAQs and getting started guides. Known issues and workarounds. Roadmap and project status updates. Community guidelines.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid issue habit is to read the title, the body, and the timeline before acting. You are listening for the requested action, the missing evidence, and the person who needs a response.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to pin an issue. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to pin an issue: You can pin up to 3 issues per repository. The next useful detail is concrete: Pinned issues are visible to everyone, even those who haven't starred or watched your repo.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, open the issue you want to pin. Then, in the right sidebar, click the three-dot menu (⋯). After that, select "Pin issue". Finally, it now appears at the top of the Issues list. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now shift from knowing the term to using it. Here is the learner-facing version. You can now pin a single comment within an issue thread to keep important information visible.

speaker: ALEX
text: That shows up in the workshop in a few specific ways. Workarounds or temporary solutions. Decisions made during discussion. Links to related issues or PRs. Status updates from maintainers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to pin a comment. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside How to pin a comment: only repository collaborators can pin comments. That matters in practice: There can be only one pinned comment per issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Find the comment you want to pin; Click the three-dot menu (⋯) on the comment; and Select "Pin comment". Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the part I would want someone to say out loud while they work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. A learner should always know what they are trying to prove before they take the next action.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where confidence starts to build. Anchor this part on Linking to Specific Lines of Code. Share precise references to code by including line numbers in GitHub URLs. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Syntax. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Syntax has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Single line: github.com/owner/repo/blob/main/file.js L42. Line range: github.com/owner/repo/blob/main/file.js L42-L58.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to create these links. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do not treat How to create these links as decoration. Line numbers are links announced as "Line 42 link" (or similar). That is not trivia. They're in the left margin of the code view.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, navigate to a file on GitHub. Then, click the line number (in screen readers: navigate to the line and activate the number link). After that, hold Shift and click another line number to select a range. Finally, copy the URL from your browser - the line numbers are automatically included. If one step does not match what you hear, stop there and re-orient.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Permalinks - Stable Links That Never Break. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Permalinks - Stable Links That Never Break is still useful because when you link to code on GitHub using a branch name (main, develop), that link can break if the code changes or the file moves. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Permalinks use the commit SHA instead of the branch name, creating a permanent snapshot link.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to create a permalink. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Put How to create a permalink into plain language. Shortcut: Y = "Yank permalink" (borrows from Vim terminology). The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: View any file on GitHub; Press Y while viewing the file - the URL changes from /blob/main/file.js to /blob/a1b2c3d4./file.js; and Copy the new URL - it now points to that specific commit. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about memorizing and more about noticing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Once the learner can name the move, the interface becomes much less intimidating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Converting Issues to Discussions. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Not every issue is a bug or feature request. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Some are questions, proposals, or open-ended conversations.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. When to convert has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: That becomes easier when you listen for these cues. Questions that don't require a code change ("How do I configure X?"). Proposals that need community feedback before becoming actionable. General discussion about the project's direction. Show-and-tell or community showcases.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to convert an issue to a discussion. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to convert an issue to a discussion: All comments and history are preserved. The next useful detail is concrete: The issue is closed and replaced with a link to the new discussion.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, open the issue. Then, in the right sidebar, click "Convert to discussion". After that, select which discussion category it belongs in. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Here is the learner-facing version. In organizations, you can mention entire teams instead of individuals: @org-name/team-name. Put another way, example: @github/accessibility notifies everyone on GitHub's accessibility team. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Why this matters. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Why this matters: permission: You can only mention teams you have visibility to. That matters in practice: Public teams in public orgs can be mentioned by anyone.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. You don't need to know who's on a team - just mention the team. Teams can subscribe to notifications as a group. CODEOWNERS files use team mentions for automatic reviewer assignment.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Anchor this part on Collapsible Sections in Markdown. Keep long issue descriptions or PR descriptions scannable by hiding details in collapsible sections.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Use for. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: accessibility note: Collapsible sections are announced as "disclosure triangles" or "expandable" regions by most screen readers. The listener should be able to check this: The summary text is always visible.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Long error messages or logs. Optional context that most readers don't need. Large screenshots or code samples. Step-by-step troubleshooting instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Do not treat Co-Authored Commits as decoration. Credit multiple people for a single commit using the Co-authored-by trailer in your commit message. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid Git habit is to know which branch you are on, what changed, and what confirmation you expect before you run the next command.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Why this matters, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Why this matters is still useful because when viewing a commit with co-authors on GitHub, screen readers announce "Co-authored-by" in the commit details.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Pair programming - both people get credit in the Git history. Crediting someone who provided the solution but didn't write the code. GitHub recognizes these trailers and shows all co-authors on the commit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the practical turn. Put Understanding Watch, Star, and Fork into plain language. Three ways to interact with a repository - each means something different.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Watch settings. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Pro tip: Star repos for discovery; Watch repos you actively contribute to.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. All activity - every issue, PR, and discussion. Participating (default) - only threads you comment on or are @mentioned in. Releases only - just new releases. Ignore - unsubscribe completely.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because the CODEOWNERS file automatically requests reviews from specific people or teams when files in their area are changed. That gives the learner a foothold: location.github/CODEOWNERS in your repository. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How it works. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How it works has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, someone opens a PR that changes files in /docs/. Then, GitHub automatically requests a review from @org-name/docs-team. After that, the PR can't be merged until the required review is approved (if branch protection requires it). Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Use for has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Ensuring domain experts review specialized code. Distributing review responsibilities. Preventing changes from being merged without appropriate oversight.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on GitHub Sponsors. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside GitHub Sponsors: support open source maintainers financially through GitHub Sponsors. That matters in practice: If a project you use has a "Sponsor" button, consider supporting them.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. How it works has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Maintainers set up a Sponsors profile. You can sponsor with a monthly recurring amount or one-time payment. GitHub doesn't take a fee (as of 2026).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Why sponsor. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Why sponsor has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Open source maintainers often work for free in their spare time. Your sponsorship helps them dedicate more time to the project. Many maintainers offer perks to sponsors (early access, prioritized issues, etc.).

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on How to sponsor. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: How to sponsor has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, visit a repository with a "Sponsor" button. Then, click the button. After that, choose a sponsorship tier. Finally, complete payment through GitHub Sponsors. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Advanced Markdown Features. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, Advanced Markdown Features is still useful because GitHub supports several powerful Markdown features beyond the basics.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put Mermaid Diagrams into plain language. graph TD A[User opens issue] -- B{Is it a bug?} B -- Yes C[Label: bug] B -- No D[Label: enhancement]. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 12. Finding More Contributions. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. After the workshop, use these resources to find your next open source contribution.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because not sure where to start after the workshop?

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the safe way to learn from that example?

speaker: ALEX
text: 16. Your Workshop Documentation - Offline Reference: Every guide from this workshop lives in your fork. The next useful detail is concrete: Clone your fork once and the complete documentation works offline - no internet required.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like git clone https://github.com/[your-username]/accessibility-agents.git. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Here is the learner-facing version. Next: Appendix Y: Workshop Materials Back: Appendix W: GitHub Pages Teaching chapter: All chapters. A good GitHub workflow is like a well-run meeting: everyone knows the topic, the next action, and who has the floor.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 38. Next in the series is episode 39, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
